findings.reachability: Unauthenticated remote attacker via HTTP request
findings.justification: Attacker-controlled superglobals (REQUEST_URI, QUERY_STRING, HTTP_USER_AGENT, HTTP_REFERER) are fed as the subject string into preg_match() patterns that contain nested alternation groups with wildcards (e.g., 'union(.*)select', '((curl_|shell_)?exec|(f|p)open|...)'), creating catastrophic backtracking conditions where a crafted input can cause PHP PCRE to spend excessive CPU time, producing a denial of service.

detection_pattern.section_name: ReDoS: user input as preg_match subject against complex alternation patterns
detection_pattern.description: Catches preg_match calls whose subject string is a raw superglobal value against a concatenated alternation regex; nested quantifiers in alternation arms can produce catastrophic backtracking on crafted inputs.

findings.location: bbq_core(), lines 46-54 (block-bad-queries.php) - apply_filters on pattern arrays
findings.reachability: Any other installed WordPress plugin (no authentication required if attacker can install a plugin, or if a co-installed plugin is compromised)
findings.justification: All five firewall pattern arrays (request_uri_items, query_string_items, user_agent_items, referrer_items, post_items) are exposed via apply_filters(), allowing any co-installed plugin to replace them with empty arrays or remove specific threat signatures, completely disabling the firewall for those request components without any capability check.

detection_pattern.description: Catches apply_filters() calls that wrap security-critical pattern arrays used for WAF/firewall matching; any co-installed plugin can empty or alter these arrays via add_filter(), bypassing the security control entirely.

findings.location: bbq_core(), lines 63-65 (block-bad-queries.php) - apply_filters on control booleans
findings.justification: The bbq_long_requests boolean and bbq_post_scanning boolean that gate entire scanning paths are exposed via apply_filters() with no access control; a co-installed plugin can add a filter returning false to disable long-request blocking and POST body scanning.

detection_pattern.section_name: Security bypass: filterable security feature toggle booleans
detection_pattern.description: Catches apply_filters() calls returning a boolean that controls whether a security feature is active; these are trivially overridden by any co-installed plugin via add_filter().

findings.location: bbq_response(), lines 137-143 (block-bad-queries.php) - apply_filters on block response headers
findings.justification: The three HTTP response headers sent by the firewall block action (bbq_header_1, bbq_header_2, bbq_header_3) are filterable, allowing a co-installed plugin to change '403 Forbidden' to '200 OK', turning every firewall block into a pass-through without modifying the plugin itself.

detection_pattern.section_name: Security bypass: filterable HTTP security response headers
detection_pattern.description: Catches header() calls whose argument is the result of apply_filters(); a co-installed plugin can silently replace a 403 block header with a 200 response, disabling the block action.

findings.reachability: Unauthenticated remote attacker via POST body with nested array values
findings.justification: When bbq_get_string() encounters a nested array it calls itself recursively at line 157 but discards the return value (no 'return' keyword), then falls through to return null; this means only the first non-array value at any depth is ever returned and all other array keys (including subsequent top-level POST keys) beyond the first nested entry are silently skipped, allowing an attacker to smuggle malicious payloads past the POST scanner by placing them in any array key after a nested value.

detection_pattern.section_name: Logic error: recursive array flattener discards return value
detection_pattern.description: Catches array-to-string helper functions that recurse into nested arrays without returning the recursive call result, causing the function to silently skip nested content and return null, allowing nested-array POST payloads to bypass WAF scanning.

findings.reachability: Attacker who can control a .po/.mo translation file (e.g., via a compromised translation package, WP filesystem write access, or a malicious co-installed language pack)
findings.justification: The _e() calls at lines 334-337 output translatable strings directly as JavaScript string literal values delimited by single quotes without esc_js() or json_encode(); a translation string containing a single quote or JavaScript injection payload would break out of the string literal and execute arbitrary JavaScript in the admin context, constituting stored XSS.

detection_pattern.section_name: XSS: unescaped translatable string in JavaScript context
detection_pattern.description: Catches _e() or __() calls whose output lands inside a JavaScript string literal without esc_js() or json_encode(); a manipulated translation file can inject arbitrary JavaScript into the admin page.

findings.reachability: Authenticated administrator only (settings page is admin-only)
findings.justification: The $href variable built by add_query_arg() is echoed into the href HTML attribute at line 205 without esc_url() wrapping; add_query_arg() does not HTML-encode its output, and the WordPress security guidelines explicitly warn that add_query_arg() output must be escaped with esc_url() before use in HTML attributes to prevent javascript: or data: URI injection.

detection_pattern.section_name: XSS: unescaped add_query_arg() output in HTML attribute
detection_pattern.description: Catches echo of add_query_arg() return value without esc_url() wrapping; the WordPress coding standards require esc_url() around add_query_arg() output in HTML contexts to prevent attribute injection.
